behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is module handoff: file discovery + filename-prefix parsing + execution sequencing. A pure unit test of sorting alone can miss wiring bugs where the runner still executes unsorted results. No real OS/network/subprocess behavior is required beyond local filesystem/module wiring, and there is no AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order filenames (for example `10_x.sql`, `2_y.sql`, `001_z.sql`). Run the migration runner with a test DB adapter/repo that records execution order, then assert applied order is numeric ascending by prefix.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on interaction between migration discovery and `_migrations` state read/write across runs. A single-function unit test is too narrow. Rejected System: behavior text only requires subsequent-run idempotency, not real subprocess/OS/network effects; local module+DB wiring is sufficient. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice against the same test database state. First run applies N migrations. Second run should execute zero migration SQL statements and leave business tables unchanged; `_migrations` row count remains N.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: This requires real transactional behavior under SQL failure plus runner stop-on-error semantics. Mocked/fake transactions can hide rollback defects. REAL-INFRA applies because correctness depends on real database transaction semantics and failure handling, not just in-process orchestration. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real PostgreSQL instance, provide migrations where one file performs a write then triggers SQL error, followed by another migration. Assert: partial writes from failing migration are absent (rolled back), later migration was not executed, and process reports failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is cross-process concurrency plus database locking/serialization behavior. Integration with single-process fakes will miss race conditions. Behavior text explicitly requires two concurrent runner instances, which disqualifies lower levels. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes simultaneously against the same PostgreSQL database and shared migrations directory. After both exit, assert each migration’s side effects appear exactly once and `_migrations` contains one record per migration (no duplicates).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is startup sequencing across components (metadata bootstrap before migration apply). A pure unit test can miss ordering at orchestration boundaries. Rejected System: behavior text is about initialization order on first run, not OS/subprocess/network conditions; local DB/module integration is enough. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh test database lacking `_migrations`. Execute runner with at least one migration file. Assert `_migrations` exists and is created before migration execution (for example by verifying no pre-check failure and expected metadata entry after first migration succeeds).
